updates.title: Synthetic users for rapid concept testing
updates.summary: Teams are using LLM-powered synthetic users to run quick concept tests before recruiting real participants, cutting early-stage iteration time by up to 60%.
updates.content: Product and research teams are increasingly turning to LLM-powered synthetic users to run fast concept tests before investing in live recruitment. Instead of waiting for screener responses and scheduling sessions, researchers prompt AI personas with the same demographics and goals as their target users, then run through flows, value propositions, and copy. Early adopters report cutting early-stage iteration time by up to 60%, while still running traditional user testing on the shortlisted concepts. The key is to treat synthetic feedback as a filter, not a replacement—use it to kill weak ideas quickly and double down on the ones that survive, then validate with real users.

source.name: Nielsen Norman Group - Accelerating Research with AI

updates.analysis:
Where it fits in the research roadmap: Use synthetic users in the early concept and discovery phase, before you write a screener or book a single participant. Ideal for testing value propositions, feature naming, and flow logic for new products (e.g. new loan types, insurance, PIX features) or for prioritising which concepts deserve real user time.

How to run it: Define 2–4 personas that mirror your real segments (e.g. first-time loan applicant, frequent PIX user, older or less digitally confident user). For each persona, prompt the synthetic user with the same scenario you’d give in a moderated test (e.g. 'You’re considering a personal loan to pay for a course'). Run through your flows and copy; capture where the synthetic user hesitates, misunderstands, or drops. Treat this as a filter: concepts that get consistent confusion or drop-off get deprioritised or revised before they ever reach real users.

Operational note: Synthetic output is not evidence. Document it as 'pre-test screening' or 'concept triage' in your research repository, and do not quote it in shareouts as user insight. Reserve real recruitment and moderated sessions for the shortlist that survives—your validity stays in the human sessions, while synthetic users reduce waste and focus recruitment on the right concepts.

updates.title: AI summarization of interview transcripts
updates.summary: Tools like Marvin and Dovetail now offer one-click summarization and theme extraction from user interview transcripts, turning hours of analysis into structured insights in minutes.
updates.content: Tools like Marvin, Dovetail, and others now offer one-click summarization and theme extraction from user interview transcripts. Researchers upload recordings or transcripts, and the tool returns structured summaries, recurring themes, and sometimes suggested quotes—turning hours of manual analysis into a first draft in minutes. Teams use these outputs as a starting point: they still review and refine, but the heavy lifting of pattern-finding is accelerated. Best practice is to keep the human in the loop for nuance and to avoid over-relying on AI for sensitive or ambiguous themes. When used well, this workflow lets researchers run more interviews without drowning in analysis.
updates.analysis:
Role in the research process: Use AI summarisation as the first pass after discovery or usability interviews. It should produce a draft structure (themes, sub-themes, candidate quotes) that you then refine—not replace—with your own judgment. That keeps synthesis faster without outsourcing interpretation on regulated or sensitive topics.

Practical workflow: After each round of interviews (e.g. 5–8 sessions per study), upload transcripts to your chosen tool. Use the AI output to build an initial affinity map or theme list; then re-read critical moments and adjust. Tag themes so they can be reused across squads (e.g. 'onboarding friction', 'trust in PIX', 'loan comprehension'). For Nubank, reserve full human synthesis for anything touching debt, fraud, identity, or regulatory risk—AI can still suggest themes, but a researcher should own the final framing and recommendations.

Quality guardrail: Keep a short checklist: 'Did the AI miss nuance or context? Did it over-generalise?' If a theme is going into a shareout or roadmap decision, trace it back to at least one real quote and one researcher note. That way AI accelerates structure and recall; researchers keep accountability for meaning and action.

updates.title: Automated usability issue detection
updates.summary: AI is being used to scan prototypes and flows to flag potential usability issues (e.g. unclear CTAs, missing feedback) before user testing, reducing redundant findings.
updates.content: AI is being used to scan prototypes and flows to flag potential usability issues before user testing. Systems analyze screens for unclear CTAs, missing feedback, inconsistent patterns, and accessibility red flags, then produce a report that researchers can use to fix obvious problems ahead of sessions. That way, user testing focuses on deeper questions instead of catching the same low-hanging fruit every time. Teams report fewer redundant findings and more time in sessions spent on behavior and reasoning. The caveat: automated checks don't replace real users—they help clean the slate so that when you do test, you learn something new.

source.name: User Interviews - The 2024 AI in UX Research Report

updates.analysis:
When to use it: Run automated checks on new or updated flows before you schedule usability sessions—especially for high-traffic or high-stakes journeys (e.g. loan application, card activation, PIX, first-time onboarding, support flows). The goal is to fix obvious usability and accessibility issues in sprint, so that when real users come in, you’re testing comprehension, trust, and edge cases instead of repeating 'the CTA was unclear' in every report.

How to integrate with Nubank’s process: Align the tool’s taxonomy with your design system and a11y standards so the report speaks the same language as design and eng. Share the report with the squad (research, design, product) and assign ownership for fixes before the next round of recruitment. After each usability study, log which issues were caught by the AI vs. found in-session—over time that helps you tune what to run automated vs. what to reserve for human testing.

Boundary: Treat the output as a pre-flight checklist, not a substitute for user testing. Real users still reveal misinterpretation, emotional friction, and context you can’t automate. Use the report to raise the floor; keep moderated sessions to understand why people behave the way they do.

updates.title: Survey design and question optimization
updates.summary: Researchers are using AI to suggest clearer survey questions, reduce bias, and adapt follow-ups based on previous answers—improving response quality and completion rates.
updates.content: Researchers are using AI to improve survey design and question quality. Tools suggest clearer wording, flag leading or double-barreled questions, and recommend shorter, more focused items. Some platforms also support adaptive follow-ups: the next question can depend on the previous answer, so respondents see only relevant branches. The result is better response quality and higher completion rates, with less manual tuning. Teams still need to review suggestions and align questions to their research goals, but AI is speeding up the draft-and-refine loop and reducing common survey pitfalls.

source.name: Typeform - Smarter surveys: A modern guide to AI-powered surveys

updates.analysis:
Where it adds value: Use AI to improve clarity, reduce bias, and shorten surveys for segment and tracking work (e.g. NPS, satisfaction, feature demand, financial habits). It’s especially useful when the topic is sensitive or complex—credit, insurance, fees—where poor wording can skew answers or confuse respondents. Use adaptive logic so follow-ups match the segment (e.g. different paths for investors vs. first-time savers), which keeps surveys relevant and completion rates higher.

Ownership and guardrails: Researchers own the research question and the final questionnaire. Use AI for structure, readability, and branching suggestions; then review every item for leading language, double-barrels, and regulatory risk. Any survey that touches terms, pricing, or legal obligations should still go through compliance and legal where required—AI does not replace that sign-off.

Operational tip: Align with whoever owns the survey platform (e.g. Research Ops or product) so that AI-improved questions live in shared templates and stay consistent across squads. Document which questions were AI-suggested vs. human-written so you can review performance and bias over time.

updates.title: Session replay + AI for behavior patterns
updates.summary: Combining session replay with AI analysis helps identify recurring drop-off patterns and confusion points at scale without manually watching hundreds of sessions.
updates.content: Combining session replay with AI analysis is helping teams spot behavior patterns at scale. Instead of manually watching hundreds of sessions, researchers use tools that aggregate replays and apply AI to detect recurring drop-offs, rage clicks, long hesitations, and navigation loops. The output is a digest of likely problem areas with example sessions to drill into. That makes it possible to prioritize which flows to study in depth and which to fix with design changes. The approach works best when paired with qualitative research—replay + AI surfaces the 'where,' and interviews or surveys explain the 'why.'
updates.analysis:
Role of research: Session replay + AI gives you the 'where'—which steps, screens, or segments show the most drop-off, hesitation, or confusion. Research’s job is to turn that into the 'why' and actionable recommendations. Use the AI digest to decide where to invest in deeper methods (e.g. moderated usability, interviews, or diary studies) and where design can iterate from behavioural signals alone.

Where to use it at Nubank: Run it on critical flows—onboarding, conversion funnels, key product flows (e.g. loan application, PIX, card activation), and support or error-recovery paths. Use the output to prioritise: which problem areas need a dedicated study vs. which can be handed to design with a short brief. That keeps research capacity focused on the most ambiguous or high-impact questions.

Ethics and compliance: Use only where Nubank’s replay and data policies allow; respect consent and minimisation. Do not rely on AI summaries alone for decisions that affect users in regulated or high-risk contexts—always pair with human review and, where needed, qualitative validation. Document how replay + AI informed your research backlog so the link between behavioural data and research questions stays clear.

updates.title: Toys, Crafts & Hobbies UX Benchmark: 5,500+ Performance Scores and 4,500+ Best Practice Examples
updates.summary: (Note: Unfortunately, e-mail and RSS don’t support advanced layouts and features. If the graphics in this article look strange, you may want to read the article in your web browser.) At Baymard, we’ve just released a new UX benchmark with 11 “Toys, Crafts & Hobbies” UX case st...
updates.content: (Note: Unfortunately, e-mail and RSS don’t support advanced layouts and features. If the graphics in this article look strange, you may want to read the article in your web browser.) At Baymard, we’ve just released a new UX benchmark with 11 “Toys, Crafts & Hobbies” UX case studies. This follows from our large-scale user testing and adds to our existing ecommerce UX benchmark. In this article, we give you a snapshot of the overall UX performance. 11 Toys, Crafts & Hobbies UX Case Studies and the Overall Performance Get Premium access to review your own site or have it audited by Baymard researchers. These are the 11 in-depth Toys, Crafts & Hobbies UX case studies. The 11 toys, crafts, and hobby sites have been manually assessed across 500+ research-based UX parameters relevant to Toys, Crafts & Hobbies, resulting in 5,500+ weighted UX performance scores and 4,500+ best practice examples from these sites. Each of the 5,500+ UX performance scores from the 11 Toys, Crafts & Hobbies case studies is summarized in the interactive scatterplot below — showing you how they perform collectively and individually: {{ scatterplot-graph: size=big + habitat=public + base-sites=collection:toys-...

updates.title: 2026 Progress: Instant UX Analysis of Your Site Now Across 209 UX Parameters (Still 95% Accuracy Rate)
updates.summary: (Note: Unfortunately, e-mail and RSS don’t support advanced layouts and features. If the graphics in this article look strange, you may want to read the article in your web browser.) The pressure on UX- and product teams to deliver results faster has never been higher, but the...
updates.content: (Note: Unfortunately, e-mail and RSS don’t support advanced layouts and features. If the graphics in this article look strange, you may want to read the article in your web browser.) The pressure on UX- and product teams to deliver results faster has never been higher, but the requirement for those results to be rooted in actual user research remains non-negotiable. At Baymard, we developed UX-Ray to allow UX designers and UX researchers to move rapidly but not recklessly, by providing an instant UX analysis that returns evidence-backed and fully traceable UX insights with a 95% accuracy rate. Over the last 4 months, we’ve achieved a 5x increase in the depth of instant UX analysis you can perform on your UX designs. UX Analysis Now Across 209 UX Parameters As of February 1st, 2026, UX-Ray can now conduct an instant UX analysis of your ecommerce site across 209 distinct UX parameters/heuristics. This is up from 39 UX parameters in October 2025. This expansion lets you move from new design frames to a comprehensive, research-backed UX audit in minutes rather than weeks. See It in Action (Video) Still with a Documented 95% Accuracy Rate Speed is irrelevant if the UX insights do not...
